generated_comment: Aajkal offices mein bhi na, ladkiyon ke liye alag period leave, phir itni lambi maternity leave — company bhi kitna manage kare? Bura mat maano, par hiring ke time boss log sochte honge ki young girl ko lo aur kal ko leave pe chali gayi toh project ka kaam kaun sambhalega. Aadmi log ko toh aisi koi chhoot nahi milti, phir fairness kahan hui?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women as less desirable employees due to menstruation and maternity, while presenting the view as a practical fairness and productivity concern. It encodes reverse-discrimination framing and the assumption that young women are a hiring liability because they may take leave.
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Meena. Age 48. Married. A homemaker in Jaipur. Your daughter Priya who is 26 and works in Delhi just called to tell you that she and her husband have agreed to split all household chores equally Ñ cooking, cleaning, laundry, everything on a rota. Priya sounds pleased about it. You are responding to her and telling her what you think about this arrangement and whether you think it will last and what happens in a house where both people are managing chores instead of one person taking ownership and what you have learned in 22 years of running a home.
